segments.title: HMS Walker's two-war destroyer career
segments.summary: The live lecture turns HMS Walker from a supposedly typical V/W-class destroyer into a case study in Royal Navy endurance, convoy work, Narvik, and anti-submarine combat.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke begins by saying he picked Walker for a date connection and expected a typical Royal Navy destroyer career, then traces a ship that served from the First World War through interwar fleet duties, early Second World War convoy and Norway operations, and the March 1941 actions against U-99 and U-100.

evidence.note: The opening frames Walker as a Royal Navy destroyer chosen for a date connection whose career proves both typical and unusually notable.
The V/W-class introduction explains the design family, late First World War construction logic, and Walker's laying down, launch, completion, and commissioning dates.
The early Second World War section places Walker in Southwest Approaches convoy work, the OB2 return collision with Vanquisher, casualties, repairs, and return to operations.
The 1940 section summarizes Norway campaign service, Walker as the last British warship to leave Narvik, convoy work, air attacks, and the encounter with U-47.
The HX 112 section describes Walker's depth-charge attacks against U-99 and U-100, including the half-hour sequence involving McIntyre, Vanoc, Kretschmer, and Schepke.
